speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 16, 1995. The survivor is Kuba Beck, B-E-C-K. The interviewer is Meta Joy Jacoby. The interview is taking place in Del Ray Beach, Florida, in the United States of America. The interview will be conducted in English. This is roll number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Meta Joy Jacoby, J-A-C-O-B-Y. This is March 16, in Del Ray Beach, Florida. I will be interviewing Kuba Beck. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Kuba Beck.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth, Kuba?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the same name at the birth, was Kuba Beck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever used a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born August 31, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which makes you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 72 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland, the city of Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a little bit about your family, and what life was like for you before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with my parents, and I had two brothers. One was two years older. One was seven years younger. We-- my father was in business. He owned a paint and hardware store. Just before the war, I finished high school and was getting ready to join my father's business to work with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school, Kuba?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to public school in Krakow, and then I went to high school, which was actually commercial school, which prepared me to do work for my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that also a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was a private Hebrew commercial school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was daily life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before the war, I really was very young, and I was with my parents, and I didn't really experience any difficulty. We live in the neighborhood where there were non-Jewish people. There was some anti-Semitism in Poland, but actually, personally, I didn't experience much of this kind. I went to public school with the non-Jewish children, and we got along quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' relations with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were-- excellent relation, actually, because being in business, we had many non-Jewish customers. And actually, my parents started the business right after the First World War, and built it up to actually was a very successful business. And before the war, we were quite comfortable. We were not very rich. However, we really were in the middle class. We owned our own big apartment house, which we lived in, and we had no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have social relations with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly-- I would say mostly they were relations-- social relation with Jewish friends. And so, we also had-- but not to a big extent.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has religion observed in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My house was very traditional. The business was closed on Saturday. We had to go with my father to a synagogue. My-- the home was kosher, and we really were quite observant. Was not Hasidic, however, very observant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Traditional Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traditional, very traditional.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a religious education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We all had a religious education. Actually, there was some Hebrew schools, religious schools. However, to our house was come-- came a man who prepared us for the synagogue prayers, and for bar mitzvah, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did mention that you did experience some anti-Semitism. Could you describe it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, especially in the latest year, and I'm talking about the years when we found that when Hitler came to power in Germany. You could already feel some more anti-Semitism in Poland. To-- just to give you an example, when we went with boys, Jewish boys, to a park or something, we used to always go in a group, because you never knew when some non-Jewish hoodlums would attack us and try to beat us up. And this-- to this extent. You could see some newspapers, some articles, literature being distributed anti-Semitism. But mainly, also, you felt during the Easter time, when some non-Jewish people, Catholics, were going out from certain churches, and depends on the priest. If he preached anti-Semitism, they used to come out and sometimes even break windows in Jewish homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were aware. However, as you have to realize, that before the war, there was no media like today. We didn't have television. Very few people had radios. But you read in the newspapers, which was always the thing what was going on in Germany, about Hitler coming to power and the law of anti-Semitism, the Nuremberg laws, and so forth. Then, when it came to Kristallnacht, this was very well-publicized. Also, there was-- I don't know if you're familiar with it, but in 1938, Hitler decided to throw out all the Polish Jews from Germany. They brought them to the Polish border, and they loaded them out by the border. Polish government didn't want to let them in. The German government didn't want to let them back, and they stayed for a while over there. And then after a while, the Polish government decided to let the people in. They were brought into-- and even some of relatives of ours, which were also in this situation, came to stay with us because they didn't have any housing for a while. So this you felt before the war already-- influence of Nazi Germany, and even in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family life change after the German invasion in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our life changed immediately, because, as you know, right after the Nazi occupied Poland and Krakow, Jews had to be identified on the street by wearing the armband with the Jewish star on it. We were exposed to harassment, to all kinds of forced labor. And however, in the 1940s, already, our father experienced the main problem, because one of the Nazis similar to Schindler came to Poland and presented-- one day, presented my father with a document, and took over our business. And became so-called troyhandler, which is, actually, got in charge. My father had to work in the business, and-- because he didn't know how to handle it. But actually, all the proceeds and everything was taken. He took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He didn't get paid. Nobody got paid, but we had to still be-- and they wouldn't give us any pay. And I was-- by the way, I was already in the business, because since I graduated high school, I joined my father's business and started working in it. However, when the Nazi took over, the business shortly afterwards, I really left, because I saw there was no room for me over there. And I had to decide to learn some kind of trade in order to survive. And being also mechanically-minded, I got myself a job as a machinist in a factory, and start learning how to be toolmaker, machines and toolmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your brothers do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, the older brother, was working before the war, was working in a bank, because he also graduate this commercial high school. My younger brother was too young. He was only 12 years old, he was too young to do any work. But my both parents were very active in the business all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother was also active in the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the troyhandler took over, she-- did I pronounce that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Troyhandler, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she remain--

speaker: interviewee
text: She remained with my father, and other people remained in the business. They had to come. It was already like a forced labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned, even prior to that, forced labor. Were you part of any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't part, because, again, I was working in my father's shop. Then when I got a job in a factory as a machinist, this was already my job. So I was lucky that I wasn't-- that was the main reason why I undertook this job as a machinist, because I felt that this might help me to stay away from these forced labor camps. My brother, then, the older brother, he lost his job in the bank. And when the war started, he got a job in the-- as a carpenter-- cabinet maker in a factory. So he had to-- everybody had to find something to do, otherwise, the Nazis would take and send you away to some forced labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of you paid for this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not paid at all. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We-- again, this was very short, because in 1941, the Nazis decided to create a ghetto in Krakow. And this changed the whole situation. For us, personally, it changed immediately, because actually, the ghetto was created in the area where we-- where the business was, and where we lived, actually, when I was born, and I grew up. Where my parents' house was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So actually, we didn't go to the ghetto, but the ghetto came to us. So this was already 1941. How did your family's life change? Was the ghetto walled at the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: This ghetto was created in one of the sections of Krakow. They ordered all the non-Jews to move out from this section. They fenced this off. They closed it up completely, and it was only a few blocks from the city. And they-- there was very, very little room for everybody to get an apartment. For us, personally, change-- it was a big change, because we had a large apartment. And we had to let in some relatives and friends into the apartment, because the people living outside, when they came in, they had to have room to stay. So in one-- in the one room, you could have as many as three or four families living in one apartment. You could have five or six or seven families living. It was very, very crowded. There was no privacy. The kitchen had to be used by everybody, and conditions were very, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my-- in our apartment, as I recall, was about, I would say 12 or 15 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had four rooms, four-room apartment with kitchen. So actually, it was five rooms. But it was very, very crowded. Conditions were bad, because the food was rationed. There was very little of it. The Nazis created some shops, factories, where all the work had to be performed for the Nazis. There was a uniform factory, a shoe factory, woodworking factory, all kinds of shops, but-- metal shop-- all the work had to be done for the Nazis. Most of the people stayed in the ghetto. However, there were some people which had the special permit to go out from the ghetto to work. I was one of them who had the permit, because the factory I worked was outside the ghetto. So I had to leave every morning and come back in the evening, after work, come back into the ghetto. Also, once in awhile, the Nazis-- quite often, actually-- the Nazis used to come in with trucks and take people out to perform all kind of work for the-- in the barracks and so forth for the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back and forth on a daily basis to your job, were you able to leave by yourself? Were you escorted?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was by myself. I had to show my permit, and I was going out from the ghetto by myself and coming by myself. There were a few people that had this kind of situation permits to leave the ghetto and come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this allow any advantage?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it was quite an advantage. Why? Because number one, I still have contact with some non-Jewish people, and having contact once in a while, I was smuggling some food into the ghetto. This was a very risky operation, because we were checked on the gate. Luckily, they never caught me smuggling in, because if I would be caught, I wouldn't be here to tell you this story, because most time, people were executed right at the gate. So actually, by having the opportunity to smuggle, I had helped my parents and my brothers to have a little extra food besides the rations. And it was an advantage to be having even contact with non-Jewish people, being on the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to have the outside job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on this job, actually, until the liquidation of ghetto, which was actually in 1943. However, in 1942, the Nazis decided that the ghetto is overcrowded, and there's not enough work for everybody. They established a selection. Everybody in the ghetto had an ID card, and had to report before a Nazi. The Nazi SS men decided-- made the decision if you're going to stay in the ghetto, or going to be resettled. And by giving you a stamp on the ID card, meant they're going to stay in the ghetto. If you didn't get the stamp, it meant that you have to be resettled. When I came before the Nazi, he asked me just a few questions, and without any problem, I got the stamp on the ID card. My parents and my brother didn't get the stamp. And it was announced that there will be a transport leaving ghetto. The people will be sent to some labor camp. They would be living in barracks. They will establish shops. They will establish work for the people. They will be taken care of and nothing to worry about. And believe me, that people believed what the Nazi propaganda was telling them. However, things were different, because in 1942, in June of 1942, one day, I went to work as usual in the morning. During the day, the Nazis closed the ghetto completely, surrounded with SS people with machine guns, and ordered all the people without the stamp on ID card to report on one square. Trucks were waiting for them. They were loaded on the trucks. They were taken to the railroad station. They were loaded in the cattle cars, and they were sent away. When I came back from work this afternoon, this evening, that's the last time I saw my family, when I went to work. And this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I still remember before even the transport, before they were sent away, I had a discussion with my parents. I said, look. I'm the only one with the stamp on ID card. Why don't I join you? It was much easier. I can- we'll be all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my parents insisted that I should stay behind, because we were quite comfortable. We had some money. We had some-- even American dollars. We had jewelry. They said, we're going to leave everything, most of the stuff with you, and when you find out where we're going to be resettled, maybe some way, somehow-- because having contact with non-Jewish people, you will be able to help us out. Maybe we'll need some help. Little did we know that actually, those transport went to extermination camp, and this was the end of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find that out? What actually happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Took me a while to find out, because people were still hoping that they are somewhere working in some labor camp. However, again, as I said, my advantage was being outside, working outside, having contact with non-Jewish people. I had a very close friend with whom I went to school. And after a while, I asked him, would you do me a favor and go and follow the trucks where the transport was sent away? And he did. After a few days, he came back, and he said the only thing I can tell you, I went as far as the town of Belzyce. This was about 300 or 400 miles away from Krakow. When I arrived over there, at the railroad station, some people, Polish people, told me, you see, there is a sidetrack from the railroad station into the forest. The transport are going into-- all the cattle cars with people-- are going into the forest. Empty cattle cars are coming out, and that's all what we can tell you. We don't know. So far, nobody came out from out there. Later on, much, much later-- even, actually, after the war-- I found out that this was an extermination camp, which was in existence only for one year. In one year, more than 600,000 Jews were destroyed in this camp. And I remained in ghetto. My life had to go on. It was very difficult at that time, being 19 years old. I had to cope with my life the best I could. I joined with one of my friends, who also lost his family, and he was a very close friend of mine. And we just stuck together and helped us-- help each other. And I was in the ghetto until liquidation of the Krakow ghetto, what was August 13, 1943. When the ghetto-- I was during the liquidation of the ghetto. Spielberg tried to show you some of the horror of the liquidation of the Krakow ghetto. But actually, I can witness-- I am a witness-- that the liquidation was much worse than actually Spielberg could show you. We are talking about liquidation of the nursery, where all the babies were killed by the SS women, and SS men. And then, the complete liquidation by ordering all the people to go on a square, report on the square. Machine guns were set up. All the people were killed-- a few thousand of them. And the bodies were brought to the cemetery, Jewish cemetery in Krakow Plaszow, and buried in mass grave. I was the lucky one, because they rounded up some people, and they sent them from the ghetto to this established concentration camp, Krakow Plaszow, on the Jewish cemetery. So I was lucky that they sent me over there to this camp. When I arrived in this camp, I was assigned to a machine shop, and I was working in this machine shop. When you saw the movie and you saw a man making hinges in this shop, I was making handcuffs for the gestapo in the same shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kuba?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: May we backtrack just a minute? Where were you during liquidation of the Krakow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was right in the ghetto, and I witnessed the horror of the liquidation. I was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't hide, because what-- before the final liquidation, as I described about the ordering the rest of the people to report in the square, just before then, they rounded up some young people. And I was-- and they sent them to this concentration camp, Krakow Plaszow. And I was one of them, which walked out from the ghetto. And actually, the concentration camp, Krakow Plaszow, was not very far from the ghetto. So actually, it was in walking distance. It was only maybe three, four miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this action take, to liquidate the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the main action was only in one day. This was the main killing. But then, it took quite a while, because the Nazis used to walk around, and there were people hiding. And they were taking-- taking them from hideouts, and they were executing them right on the spot. And then, also, they were-- later on, they brought some people from the Krakow Plaszow camp in order to clean up all the dead people, and load furniture on trucks, and the Nazis were taking away actually to-- they used to call it Zalburg Commando. There was a commando for cleaning up the ghetto. And they cleaned up. I wasn't one of them, because I was right away assigned to this-- to the shop, the metal shop in Plaszow. And I was working over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you saw with your own eyes the liquidation of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I saw part of it. It was quite a horror-- especially, we were assembled close to the hospital, where the babies were liquidated. And they were throwing little babies through the window. They were hitting the babies, grabbing them by the legs and hitting the-- head over by the wall, because they were yelling-- it's a waste to-- not to waste a bullet on the babies. And many, many other horrors, when they were catching people in hiding, and they were shooting them right on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Krakow-Plaszow--

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Making handcuffs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I was working over there. Now, the condition in Krakow Plaszow, again, I would say that Spielberg tried to show quite a bit how Amon Goeth was-- Amon Goeth was actually in action, and how he was killing and harassing and executing. Every day we had execution. The food was rationed very little. The hygiene conditions were just terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there less food than in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Less food than the ghetto, definitely. That's it?
